title: Full Feature Integration Sprint
problem: The app currently renders a basic feature tree but none of the designed features are actually built: the About section is static, the design system tokens aren't fully applied, lineage collapse/expand doesn't exist, the ecosystem overview has no visual treatment, and focus/navigation mode is unimplemented. The feature.json files describe what to build — now the app needs to catch up to its own documentation.
analysis: The feature.json files across the project are the implementation spec. Each feature's status field drives the work queue: active = build it now, draft = spec only, frozen = locked, deprecated = skip. The integration sprint works through every active feature in dependency order — design tokens first (feat-2026-003), then app shell improvements (feat-2026-002), then About section (feat-2026-004), then ecosystem section (feat-2026-006 + children), then lineage collapse (feat-2026-005), then navigation/focus mode (feat-2026-014). Each feature becomes a testable increment: build it, verify against its successCriteria, mark done.

decisions.decision: Work in dependency order, not feature-key order
decisions.rationale: Design tokens (003) must land before any component styling. Collapse behaviour (005) must land before focus mode (014) builds on top of it. Building out of order creates rework.
alternativesConsidered: All at once — impossible to test incrementally

decisions.decision: Use each feature's successCriteria as the acceptance test
decisions.rationale: The successCriteria field was written to be specific and testable. Using it as the definition of done closes the loop between documentation and implementation.
alternativesConsidered: Ad-hoc testing — no clear completion signal
Separate test spec — duplicates what's already in the feature.json

decisions.decision: successCriteria rendered in the expanded card view
decisions.rationale: Makes the app self-describing — visitors can see not just what was built but how we knew it was done. The showcase shows its own acceptance criteria.
alternativesConsidered: Only internal / not rendered — wastes the field for showcase purposes

decisions.decision: types.ts extended with successCriteria and domain fields
decisions.rationale: Both fields are present in the feature.json files. Exposing them in the TypeScript interface ensures they're rendered in the card and type-checked at build time.
alternativesConsidered: Keep types narrow, access via JSON cast — loses type safety

decisions.decision: features.ts dead code (byKey, rooted variables) removed during integration
decisions.rationale: The integration sprint is a natural point to fix known issues documented in feat-2026-002's knownLimitations. Removing dead code is zero-risk during a full rewrite of the consuming component.
alternativesConsidered: Leave it — tech debt grows; the knownLimitations field already called it out

implementation:
Integration order:
1. feat-2026-003 — apply all CSS design tokens to index.css and App.css
2. feat-2026-002 — About component wired into App.tsx above the feature list
3. feat-2026-004 — About section styled with design tokens, links verified
4. feat-2026-006..013 — ecosystem features rendered in the tree; no special UI needed beyond correct lineage
5. feat-2026-005 — collapse/expand toggle on parent cards, child-count badge on collapsed parents
6. feat-2026-014 — focus mode via ?focus= URL param, breadcrumb component, collapse-all scoped shortcut
Each increment is verified against the feature's successCriteria before moving to the next.
knownLimitations: No automated test suite — successCriteria verification is manual visual inspection
successCriteria: Every active feature in the project renders correctly in the running app. Design tokens are applied consistently. The About section appears above the feature list. Collapse/expand works on any feature with children. Focus mode isolates a feature + ancestors + children via ?focus= URL. The ecosystem sub-tree (feat-2026-006 through 013) is navigable.

lineage.spawnReason: The app shell gave the app a runnable scaffold; this sprint is what makes that scaffold showcase-worthy — wiring every designed feature into the live app.
